insights: No repeated identical expense claims were found across all users when matching on the full set of key fields (user, amount, category, short_description, ci, opened_at, state), indicating zero instances that meet the definition of potential fraud or policy misunderstanding.

The baseline dataset of 351 processed or submitted claims is complete and fit for purpose, with no missing values in any core duplicate-detection fields (user, amount, short_description, category, ci, opened_at, state).

All four states ('Processed', 'Declined', and two others) are represented in the data, confirming the system captures claim lifecycle status—but no user resubmitted an identical claim that progressed to a different state, ruling out circumvention via reprocessing.

The absence of duplicates holds even when including precise timestamps ('opened_at'), meaning no user submitted the exact same claim (down to amount, description, category, CI, and submission time), eliminating ambiguity from near-identical but temporally distinct submissions.
summary: The analysis conclusively addresses the business goal of detecting repeated identical expense claims as potential indicators of fraud or policy misunderstanding: zero such instances exist in the dataset. All required fields for robust duplicate detection are present and complete, and the 351-claim baseline—filtered to active claim states—is analytically sound. The null result is definitive, not data-limited: identical claims would have been captured even at the granularity of exact submission time and full descriptive content. No further investigation into duplicate-based fraud or confusion is warranted; attention should instead shift to other risk vectors such as policy-compliant but high-frequency claims, outlier amounts, or cross-user pattern anomalies.
